Casali discusses the Nisus and Euryalus episode in the Aeneid; the nocturnal massacre in Statius, Th. 10; and the Doloneia in Silius Italicus's Punica. Duval observes that ancient evidence for the Sortes Vergilianae is attested only in the Historia Augusta, whereas the Sortes Homericae are witnessed in three papyri. Fratantuono notices that the first book of Quintus of Smyrna's Posthomerica manipulates and reverses the plot of Aen. 11. Houghton cites Tibullus 1.3.35-50, Horace, Carm. saec. 57-60 and Carm. 4.2.37-40, and passages from Vergil himself that demonstrate early responses to his fourth Eclogue. López Romero notes that Seneca's maiora notis monstra alludes to nota maior imago at Aen. 2.773. Seidman observes that Tacitus's approach to the Teutoburg episode has much in common with Aeneas's viewing of the Trojan War mural at the Temple of Juno in Carthage (Aen. 1.451-65). Ware focuses on the fourth-century corpus of the Panegyrici Latini, whose orators "acknowledge Virgil as magnus poeta and cite him directly, particularly to evoke battle scenes, while also drawing on his work generally for poetic language and epic phrasing" (1). Lagioia, with a view to a new edition of Servius's commentary, examines the first book of the Georgics to provide critical reflection on the edition of G. Thilo (1881-87) and on the criteria to be adopted for a new text. Pirovano's is the second part of a study of the manuscripts Bern, Burgerbibliothek 165 (T), and Vatican, Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, Vat. Lat. 1570 (F). Racine discusses the role of Greek in Servius's commentary and in the Roman education and culture of his day. Murgia and Kaster's edition is the first addition to the Harvard Servius to appear in over fifty years. Stok speculates that the word virgineum in the context of an acrostic at Georg. 1.430 may have suggested his ancient nickname Parthenias that, according to Donatus in his Vita Vergili, was assigned to Vergil in Naples. Urîa finds a previously undetected connection between the earliest approaches to what is known Vergilian Bibliography - 199 as the septimus casus and those found in Servius's commentaries on Vergil, approaches that differ from those followed in the later grammarians. Vallat (2017) analyzes the transmission of Varro in late antique Vergil commentaries, discussing the reliability of authors' names and titles of works in citations, forms of quotation, complications entailed by manuscript transmission, and other issues. Appleton and Robinson write on the Anglo-Latin hagiography Vita Sancti Guthlaci, concerning the hermit Guthlac of Crowland (d. 714), written by a certain Felix ca. 730-40. Davies discusses the reception of the magical and prophetic Vergil of popular legend and the place of the Eclogues, Georgics, and Aeneid in Wales from the Middle Ages on. Florano and Zorzetti discuss Giovanni Boccaccio (1313-75). Iafrate touches on the medieval literary persona of Vergil as a magician who could capture demons in a bottle. Ibáñez Chacón discusses the Liber monstrorum, a late seventh- or early eighthcentury catalog of marvelous creatures whose mythological narratives are drawn largely from Vergil. Martin "focuses on Heinrich von Veldeke's Eneasroman (ca. 1186), the first courtly romance written in German. It is an adaptation of the Old French Roman d'Eneas (c. 1160), itself an adaptation of Vergil's Aeneid" (18). O'Sullivan discusses Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Auct. F.2.8. Perucchi discusses Francesco Petrarch (1304-74). Ratkowitsch demonstrates that the Latin poem Waltharius depends on the Aeneid for individual motifs and overall structure. Ricklin discusses the influence of the medieval legends about Vergil on William of Malmesbury's (1080-1143) depiction of Sylvester II as a necromantic philosopher. Abbamonte observes that the humanist Giuniano Maio (1430-93) derived his quotations from Vergil from second-hand sources (Servius, Nonius, Gellius, and others). Abbamonte observes that the humanist Giuniano Maio (1430-93) derived
his quotations from Vergil from second-hand sources (Servius, Nonius,
Gellius, and others). Auger discusses Cristoforo Landino (1424-98). Basu
considers the Arcadia of Philip Sidney (1554-86) as a form of fan fiction
of the Eclogues. Béhar discusses the influence of Eurydice in Georg. 4 on
the character of Elisa in the third Eclogue of Garcilaso de la Vega (1501-
36). Cappelli analyzes quotations from Vergil in Neapolitan political
treatises by Giovanni Pontano (1426-1503), Francesco Patrizi (1529-97),
and Giuniano Maio. Casanova-Robin considers the meaning behind the
etiological tale about Vergil's birth devised by Pontano in elegy 1.14 of his
Eridanus. Deramaix discusses the imitation of Vergil in the writings of
Jacopo Sannazaro (1458-1530). Escobar Borrego writes about the concept
of Vergil's Arcadia and its influence on Don Alonso Pérez de Guzmán
(1550-1615) and suggests that Soledades and Retrato by his son Pedro
Espinosa (1578-1650) are best understood in that context. Disagreeing
with twentieth-century views that take Lucan's Bellum civile as a parodic
subversion of Vergil, Facchini argues that Dante regards the epics of Vergil
and Lucan as consonant with each other. Ferreri offers reflections on
sources pertaining to the Vergilian exegesis of Aulo Giano Parrasio (Aulus
Janus Parrhasius) (1470-1522) in order to focus on his commentaries on
Horace's Ars poetica, on Claudianus's De raptu Proserpinae, and on his
own De rebus per epistolam quaesitis. Foley compares Cristoforo Landino's
(1424-98) interpretation of the Aeneid, which he read as a moral allegory of
the soul's progression to philosophical wisdom, with the similar interpretive
scheme that Marsilio Ficino (1433-99) applied to the dialogues of Plato.
Gevrey discusses Les exilés de la cour d'Auguste by Marie-Catherine de
Villedieu (1640-83). Hampton writes about Os Lusíadas by Luís de Camões
(1524-80). Holmes notes that the embrace between Vergil and Sordello, a
thirteenth-century troubadour born, like Vergil, in Mantua, "binds with
Vergilian Bibliography - 205
love … all of Italy's previously scattered languages, literary genres, and
political ideologies" (106). Houghton, "Virgil," considers "the contribution
made by poetic imagery derived from the work of Virgil," in particular from
"the fourth Eclogue, to contemporary formulations of the concept of the
Renaissance itself" (203). Laterza considers Vergil's use of pathetic fallacy in
the cave scene in Aen. 4 and how it was reworked in Renaissance literature.
Lovatt explores the use of Vergil's underworld and the reworking of Dante
in Renaissance Florence by Ugolino Verino (1438-1516) in his epic poem
Carlias. MacCarthy's essay focuses on the "poet-musician Iopas [at Aen.
1.740-47], the subject of his banquet song and the increased interest
in him as a musician and poet by late medieval and early Renaissance
writers" (85-86). Marshall writes that in 1592 "Robert Greene warned
his fellow playwrights that 'there is an upstart Crow, beautified with our
feathers, that with his Tigers hart wrapped in a players hyde, supposes he
is as well able to blast out a blank verse as the best of us." Greene almost
certainly meant William Shakespeare as the upstart crow, and drew the
image from Vergil's cornix … improba at Georg. 1.388-89, which he must
have known in Latin. Marsico writes on Lorenzo Valla (1407-57). Martín
Romano writes about the major influence of the Aeneid on the epic poem
Nuevo Mundo y conquista by Francisco de Terrazas (1540-1600), the first
Mexican poet. Martindale considers the period from Henry Howard, Earl
of Surrey (1517-47) to Alfred, Lord Tennyson (1809-92). Mauriello takes
Arcadia and Eclogae piscatoriae by Sannazaro as a starting point to examine
vernacular bucolic in Italy during the sixteenth century, focusing ultimately
on the poetry of Luigi Tansillo (1510-68) and Berardino Rota (1508-75).
Michon discusses Pierre-Valentin Faydit's (1644-1709) reception of Vergil.
Miletti observes that an elegy by Francesco Peto da Fondi (1480?-after
1531), following Servius's commentary, offers an antiquarian inquiry on
Amyclae, the Spartan colony in southern Latium mentioned at Aen. 10.564.
Molins discusses Joachim Du Bellay (1522-60). Mussio maintains that
Dante's interpretation of Vergil's Amata in Purg. 17 as an example of wrath
is consistent with his treatment of Amata in Epist. 7. Nassichuk focuses
on an elegiac poem by Nicolas Chesneau (1521-81). Pérez-Abadin Barro
discusses the Églogas pastoriles, modeled on Vergil's Eclogues, by Pedro de
Padilla (1540-99). Pietras discusses The Shepheardes Calender by Edmund
Spenser (1552-99). Potter writes on Christopher Marlowe (1564-93).
Pugh focuses on Edmund Spenser's (1552-99) acknowledged debt in his
pastoral poems to Vergil's Eclogues. Quint writes on Angelo Ambrogini
(Poliziano) (1454-94). Reeser discusses Michel de Montaigne (1533-92).
Rogerson writes about Maffeo Vegio's 1428 Supplement to the Aeneid, the
"extraordinarily successful 'Aeneid 13'" (119). Wingfield examines the

Abbamonte observes that the humanist Giuniano Maio (1430-93) derived his quotations from Vergil from second-hand sources (Servius, Nonius, Gellius, and others). Auger discusses Cristoforo Landino (1424-98). Basu considers the Arcadia of Philip Sidney (1554-86) as a form of fan fiction of the Eclogues. Béhar discusses the influence of Eurydice in Georg. 4 on the character of Elisa in the third Eclogue of Garcilaso de la Vega (1501- 36). Cappelli analyzes quotations from Vergil in Neapolitan political treatises by Giovanni Pontano (1426-1503), Francesco Patrizi (1529-97), and Giuniano Maio. Casanova-Robin considers the meaning behind the etiological tale about Vergil's birth devised by Pontano in elegy 1.14 of his Eridanus. Deramaix discusses the imitation of Vergil in the writings of Jacopo Sannazaro (1458-1530). Escobar Borrego writes about the concept of Vergil's Arcadia and its influence on Don Alonso Pérez de Guzmán (1550-1615) and suggests that Soledades and Retrato by his son Pedro Espinosa (1578-1650) are best understood in that context. Disagreeing with twentieth-century views that take Lucan's Bellum civile as a parodic subversion of Vergil, Facchini argues that Dante regards the epics of Vergil and Lucan as consonant with each other. Ferreri offers reflections on sources pertaining to the Vergilian exegesis of Aulo Giano Parrasio (Aulus Janus Parrhasius) (1470-1522) in order to focus on his commentaries on Horace's Ars poetica, on Claudianus's De raptu Proserpinae, and on his own De rebus per epistolam quaesitis. Foley compares Cristoforo Landino's (1424-98) interpretation of the Aeneid, which he read as a moral allegory of the soul's progression to philosophical wisdom, with the similar interpretive scheme that Marsilio Ficino (1433-99) applied to the dialogues of Plato. Gevrey discusses Les exilés de la cour d'Auguste by Marie-Catherine de Villedieu (1640-83). Hampton writes about Os Lusíadas by Luís de Camões (1524-80). Holmes notes that the embrace between Vergil and Sordello, a thirteenth-century troubadour born, like Vergil, in Mantua, "binds with Vergilian Bibliography - 205 love … all of Italy's previously scattered languages, literary genres, and political ideologies" (106). Houghton, "Virgil," considers "the contribution made by poetic imagery derived from the work of Virgil," in particular from "the fourth Eclogue, to contemporary formulations of the concept of the Renaissance itself" (203). Laterza considers Vergil's use of pathetic fallacy in the cave scene in Aen. 4 and how it was reworked in Renaissance literature. Lovatt explores the use of Vergil's underworld and the reworking of Dante in Renaissance Florence by Ugolino Verino (1438-1516) in his epic poem Carlias. MacCarthy's essay focuses on the "poet-musician Iopas [at Aen. 1.740-47], the subject of his banquet song and the increased interest in him as a musician and poet by late medieval and early Renaissance writers" (85-86). Marshall writes that in 1592 "Robert Greene warned his fellow playwrights that 'there is an upstart Crow, beautified with our feathers, that with his Tigers hart wrapped in a players hyde, supposes he is as well able to blast out a blank verse as the best of us." Greene almost certainly meant William Shakespeare as the upstart crow, and drew the image from Vergil's cornix … improba at Georg. 1.388-89, which he must have known in Latin. Marsico writes on Lorenzo Valla (1407-57). Martín Romano writes about the major influence of the Aeneid on the epic poem Nuevo Mundo y conquista by Francisco de Terrazas (1540-1600), the first Mexican poet. Martindale considers the period from Henry Howard, Earl of Surrey (1517-47) to Alfred, Lord Tennyson (1809-92). Mauriello takes Arcadia and Eclogae piscatoriae by Sannazaro as a starting point to examine vernacular bucolic in Italy during the sixteenth century, focusing ultimately on the poetry of Luigi Tansillo (1510-68) and Berardino Rota (1508-75). Michon discusses Pierre-Valentin Faydit's (1644-1709) reception of Vergil. Miletti observes that an elegy by Francesco Peto da Fondi (1480?-after 1531), following Servius's commentary, offers an antiquarian inquiry on Amyclae, the Spartan colony in southern Latium mentioned at Aen. 10.564. Molins discusses Joachim Du Bellay (1522-60). Mussio maintains that Dante's interpretation of Vergil's Amata in Purg. 17 as an example of wrath is consistent with his treatment of Amata in Epist. 7. Nassichuk focuses on an elegiac poem by Nicolas Chesneau (1521-81). Pérez-Abadin Barro discusses the Églogas pastoriles, modeled on Vergil's Eclogues, by Pedro de Padilla (1540-99). Pietras discusses The Shepheardes Calender by Edmund Spenser (1552-99). Potter writes on Christopher Marlowe (1564-93). Pugh focuses on Edmund Spenser's (1552-99) acknowledged debt in his pastoral poems to Vergil's Eclogues. Quint writes on Angelo Ambrogini (Poliziano) (1454-94). Reeser discusses Michel de Montaigne (1533-92). Rogerson writes about Maffeo Vegio's 1428 Supplement to the Aeneid, the "extraordinarily successful 'Aeneid 13'" (119). Wingfield examines the 206 - Shirley Werner correspondences that might be drawn between a set of extracts from Julius Caesar Scaliger's (1494-1558) Epidorpides (published posthumously in 1573) copied in the Ruthven manuscript (Edinburgh, University Library, MS Dc.1.43) and the subsequent translation of Virgil's Aeneid by Gavin Douglas (1474-1522). Séris writes on the neo-Latin poet Girolamo Angeriano (Hieronymus Angerianus, 1480-1512). Toscano writes about six eclogues by Giano Anisio (1465-1640) in his Varia poemata that blend Vergilian and contemporary influences. Tucker's subject is the sixteenth- to seventeenth-century Latin verse cento based preponderantly upon Vergil. Tufano considers a description of a firework display by Gabriele Altilio (1436-1501) that is modeled on Vergil's description of the burning of Troy. Calvert, Ian. Kantzios draws attention to François Fénelon's (1651-1715) novel The Adventures of Telemachus, Son of Ulysses (1699), which drew on the setting of Vergil's underworld to criticize the regime of Louis XIV. Vince considers Miklós Zrínyi (1620-64). O'Briain, Katarina. "Dryden's Georgic Fictionality." Eighteenth-Century Fiction 30 (2018): 317-38. Sjödin, Alfred. "Saint-Lambert, Oxenstierna et la 'mémoire du genre' géorgiche." Revue d'histoire littéraire de la France 117 (2017): 529-36. O'Briain takes Dryden's 1697 translation of the Georgics as a case study to recover "a common history of georgic and fictionality that resides in [Vergil's and Dryden's] shared attention to craft labour" (317). Sjödin uses Bakhtin's theory of the mémoire du genre to study the influence of the Saisons by JeanFrançois de Saint-Lambert (1716-1803) in the work of the Swedish poet Johan Gabriel Oxenstierna (1750-1818), both of which stand as examples of Vergilian-inspired georgics. Frost considers the question whether death provides a generative force in politics and points to the opinion of Hannah Arendt (1906-75), who felt that birth, not death, equipped us for the task of beginning and that the Aeneid shows the best understanding of this process. Marshall considers the place of Vergil in the poetic imagination of Tony Harrison (1937-). Nolden analyzes how Aimé Césaire (1913-2008) in his epic poem Cahier d'un retour au pays natal appropriates ancient epic as a point of departure for postcolonial writing, while also reflecting the subversive farce of Plautus. raw_text: Geburtstages, edited by Stefanie Nawracala and Robert Nawracala. Aachen: Shaker, 2015. Nollé, Johannes. "Beiträge zur kleinasiatischen Münzkunde und Geschichte. 14, Antandros am Südfuss des Idagebirges: Eine monetäre Vergil-Illustration und die göttliche Patronin der Stadt." Gephyra 14 (2017): 35-100. Suerbaum, Werner. Vergils Epos als Drama: Die Gattungstransformation der Inclyta Aeneis in der Tragicocomoedia des Johannes Lucienberger, Frankfurt 1576. Tübingen: Narr Francke Attempto, 2018. Vitela, Lisa Boutin. "Virgilian Imagery and the Maiolica of the Mantuan Court." In Houghton and Sgarbi, Virgil and Renaissance Culture, 49-62. Frick discusses the 1543 Worms edition of the Aeneid translation by Thomas Murner (1475-1537) into German, which included frontispieces that represented the content of each book visually and can only be understood by a reader who knows the entire Aeneid. Maass considers the art nouveau of Adolphe Giraldon (1855-1933) and the influence of Ecl. 1 and 4. Nollé writes about a medallion coined in Antandros in the Troad during the reign of Severus Alexandrinus that depicts the flight of Aeneas from Troy with Anchises and Ascanius. Vitela considers the maiolica dishes painted by Nicola da Urbino (active ca. 1520-1537/8) that were illustrated with classical narratives, including scenes inspired by the Aeneid and Ovid's Metamorphoses. Duke University
